turns.content: right so I've been looking into something and I want to think it through a bit before I commit to anything.
Sure, happy to help you think through it. What are you considering?

theres an equine surgery certificate I've had my eye on. been seeing more horse work come through the practice lately and theres proper demand for it up here. not many vets in the highlands that do equine surgery.

That makes sense -- if there's demand in your area and not many vets filling that niche, it could be a smart move both professionally and financially. What does the certificate involve? Is it a course you'd do alongside your current work or would you need to take time off?

its a six month course. run through edinburgh. some of it is distance learning and some is practical blocks where you'd go down for a week at a time. so I could prob keep working through most of it but the practical weeks would mean I'm out of action for farm calls.

That's a reasonable structure -- the distance learning keeps you earning, and the practical blocks are intense but finite. How many practical weeks are there over the six months? And would your practice be able to cover your farm calls while you're away, or would that be a problem?

think its four practical blocks of a week each spread across the six months. coverage shouldnt be a huge issue. we've got locums we can bring in and tbh the other vets can absorb a week here and there. its more the cost thats giving me pause.
What's the cost looking like? And is there any funding or support available -- from your employer, professional development grants, anything like that?

somewhere around eight grand all in. plus travel and accommodation for the edinburgh blocks. so prob closer to ten when you factor everything. employer might chip in a bit but I wouldnt bank on it being more than a token gesture tbh.

Ten thousand is a significant investment, no question. The way to think about it is probably: would the equine work you'd pick up afterward cover that cost within a reasonable timeframe? If there's genuine unmet demand in the Highlands for equine surgery and you'd be one of the few vets offering it, the return could be fairly quick. Horse owners tend to pay well because the alternative is transporting a horse a long distance to find a specialist.

aye thats the thing. equine work pays well. way better than cattle and sheep per call. and theres estates up here with proper money that would rather have someone local than trailer a horse to glasgow or edinburgh. so the money side could work out fine in theory.

It sounds like the business case is solid. The demand is there, the money in equine work is better, and being the local option for Highland estates is a real competitive advantage. What's the part that's still making you hesitate? Is it just the upfront cost, or is there something else?

honestly I think its just the cost and the fact that I've never really done much horse work. cattle and sheep is what I know. horses are a different beast entirely. literally. but aye the demand is there and it would be good money so prob worth at least applying and seeing what happens.

That's a sensible approach -- applying doesn't commit you to anything, and the process of putting an application together might help you crystallize how serious you are about it. And the fact that horses are different from what you know is exactly why the certificate exists. You've got the surgical fundamentals from your cattle work; the course would build the equine-specific knowledge on top of that. When's the next intake?

think its september. so I've got time to think about it. might look into whether theres any bursaries or professional development funds I can tap into because ten grand out of pocket is a lot. but aye. worth exploring. cheers for helping me think it through.

No problem. It sounds like a well-reasoned opportunity -- real demand, good earning potential, and a manageable course structure. Definitely worth checking the bursary angle; the RCVS and BVA both have professional development funding schemes, and some of the Highland development bodies have grants for rural professionals upskilling. Good luck with it.
